meta.title: REST API
meta.description: Review checklist for REST API design, HTTP semantics, and endpoint review

items.text: Are URLs using plural nouns for resource names (not verbs)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: REST URLs should name resources (nouns), not actions (verbs). Use HTTP methods for actions: GET /users, not GET /getUsers.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Verb-based URLs create inconsistency and redundancy. Every team invents different verbs. Resource-based URLs are predictable.
baseContent.howToVerify: Do URLs use nouns? Are they plural? Are there verb-based URLs like /createUser or /deleteItem?
baseContent.exampleComment: `POST /createOrder` should be `POST /orders`. The HTTP method already conveys the action.

codeExamples.label: Good URLs
codeExamples.code:
GET    /users          # list
POST   /users          # create
GET    /users/123      # get one
PATCH  /users/123      # update
DELETE /users/123      # delete
GET    /users/123/orders  # nested resource

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use plural nouns for resources. Let HTTP methods express the action.

items.text: Is the URL path structure consistent across endpoints?

baseContent.whatItMeans: URL patterns should be consistent: `/users/{id}/orders` not sometimes `/user/{id}/order` and sometimes `/users/{userId}/orders-list`.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Inconsistent paths make the API hard to learn and integrate with. Developers constantly check the docs instead of guessing correctly.
baseContent.howToVerify: Are path segments consistently plural? Are ID parameter names consistent? Is nesting consistent?
baseContent.exampleComment: Some endpoints use `/user/{id}` and others use `/users/{userId}`. Standardize to `/users/{userId}` everywhere.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Consistency matters more than any specific convention. Pick patterns and stick to them.

items.text: Are query parameters used for filtering, sorting, and pagination (not path segments)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Filtering, sorting, and pagination belong in query parameters, not path segments. Path segments identify resources.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Path segments like `/users/active/sorted-by-name/page/2` create an explosion of routes and aren't RESTful.
baseContent.howToVerify: Are filters in query params (`?status=active&sort=name`)? Or in the path (`/users/active`)?
baseContent.exampleComment: Move the filter from the path to a query parameter: `/users?status=active&sort=created_at&order=desc`.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Path segments = resource identity. Query parameters = filtering, sorting, pagination.

items.text: Is the API versioned appropriately?

baseContent.whatItMeans: APIs should be versioned to allow breaking changes without disrupting existing clients. Common approaches: URL path (/v1/), header, or query param.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without versioning, any breaking change forces all clients to update simultaneously, which is often impossible.
baseContent.howToVerify: Is there a version in the URL or headers? Is the versioning strategy documented? Are there plans for the next version?
baseContent.exampleComment: This API has no versioning. Add `/v1/` prefix so breaking changes can be introduced in `/v2/` without disrupting existing clients.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Version your API from day one. URL-based versioning (/v1/) is the most visible and easiest to implement.

items.text: Are sensitive data kept out of URLs (use headers or body)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: URLs appear in browser history, server logs, proxy logs, and Referer headers. Sensitive data in URLs is easily leaked.
baseContent.whyItMatters: API keys, tokens, passwords, and PII in URLs get logged everywhere, creating a security audit nightmare.
baseContent.howToVerify: Are there query parameters containing tokens, passwords, or PII? Should they be in headers or the request body instead?
baseContent.exampleComment: `/users?api_key=sk-abc123` leaks the API key in logs. Use the Authorization header instead.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never put secrets or PII in URLs. Use Authorization headers for tokens, request bodies for sensitive data.

items.text: Is the correct HTTP method used for the operation?

baseContent.whatItMeans: HTTP methods have defined semantics: GET=read, POST=create, PUT=replace, PATCH=partial update, DELETE=remove.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Using POST for everything loses the benefits of HTTP: caching (GET), idempotency (PUT/DELETE), and clear intent.
baseContent.howToVerify: Does the endpoint use the right method? Is GET used for reads (not modifications)? Is DELETE used for removal?
baseContent.exampleComment: This endpoint uses `POST /users/123/delete`. Use `DELETE /users/123` instead — the HTTP method conveys the intent.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use HTTP methods as designed. GET for reads, POST for creation, PUT/PATCH for updates, DELETE for removal.

items.text: Are HTTP status codes used correctly and consistently?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Status codes communicate the result: 200=OK, 201=Created, 204=No Content, 400=Bad Request, 404=Not Found, 409=Conflict, 500=Server Error.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Wrong status codes break client logic. Returning 200 for errors makes error handling impossible. Returning 500 for bad input hides bugs.
baseContent.howToVerify: Is 201 returned for creation? 204 for deletion? 404 for missing resources? 400 for validation errors? 409 for conflicts?
baseContent.exampleComment: This endpoint returns 200 for successful creation. Use 201 Created with a Location header pointing to the new resource.

codeExamples.label: Correct codes
codeExamples.code:
POST /users → 201 Created + Location: /users/456
GET /users/999 → 404 Not Found
PATCH /users/123 (invalid) → 400 Bad Request
DELETE /users/123 → 204 No Content
POST /users (duplicate email) → 409 Conflict

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use status codes precisely. They're the API's primary error communication channel.

items.text: Are PUT and DELETE idempotent?

baseContent.whatItMeans: PUT and DELETE must be idempotent — calling them multiple times has the same effect as calling once. POST is not idempotent.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Non-idempotent DELETE means retrying a failed delete request could delete a different resource. Non-idempotent PUT corrupts data.
baseContent.howToVerify: Does calling DELETE twice on the same resource cause an error or side effect? Does PUT always produce the same result?
baseContent.exampleComment: DELETE /orders/123 returns 404 on the second call. It should return 204 (or 404 is acceptable) — the key is no side effects on retry.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: PUT and DELETE must be safe to retry. If a client retries, the result should be the same.

items.text: Do GET requests have no side effects?

baseContent.whatItMeans: GET requests must be safe — they should not modify data. Web crawlers, preloaders, and caches assume GET is safe.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A GET request that modifies data gets triggered by crawlers, browser preloading, and link previews, causing unintended mutations.
baseContent.howToVerify: Does any GET endpoint modify data, create records, or trigger actions? These should be POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE.
baseContent.exampleComment: `GET /orders/123/cancel` modifies data via a GET request. Use `POST /orders/123/cancel` instead.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: GET must never modify data. Browsers, crawlers, and CDNs assume it's safe to call GET at any time.

items.text: Is the response format consistent across all endpoints?

baseContent.whatItMeans: All endpoints should return the same response envelope. Whether it's `{ data, meta }` or direct objects, be consistent.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Inconsistent response formats force clients to write per-endpoint parsing logic, increasing complexity and bugs.
baseContent.howToVerify: Do all endpoints use the same response structure? Is there a standard envelope? Are list and single-item responses consistent?
baseContent.exampleComment: Some endpoints return `{ data: [...] }` and others return bare arrays. Standardize on one response envelope.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Pick a response format and use it everywhere. Consistency saves more time than cleverness.

items.text: Are list endpoints paginated?

baseContent.whatItMeans: List endpoints must be paginated to prevent unbounded responses. Support page-based or cursor-based pagination.
baseContent.whyItMatters: An unpaginated endpoint returning 1M records crashes clients, exhausts server memory, and times out.
baseContent.howToVerify: Do list endpoints accept pagination parameters? Is there a default and maximum page size? Is total count available?
baseContent.exampleComment: The `GET /orders` endpoint returns all orders with no pagination. Add `?page=1&per_page=20` with a max of 100.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Every list endpoint needs pagination. Default to 20 items, max 100. Include total count in metadata.

items.text: Are filtering and sorting parameters well-defined?

baseContent.whatItMeans: List endpoints should support filtering by relevant fields and sorting with a clear syntax.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without standardized filtering, clients request all data and filter client-side, wasting bandwidth and server resources.
baseContent.howToVerify: Are filterable fields documented? Is the sort syntax clear (e.g., `sort=created_at&order=desc`)? Are invalid filters rejected?
baseContent.exampleComment: Add filtering support: `GET /orders?status=active&created_after=2024-01-01&sort=-created_at` (prefix - for descending).
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Define filterable fields and sort syntax. Reject unknown filter fields with 400.

items.text: Are Content-Type and Accept headers handled correctly?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Requests should specify Content-Type (what the body contains) and Accept (what the client wants). The server should respect both.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Ignoring Content-Type leads to parsing errors. Ignoring Accept means clients get unexpected formats.
baseContent.howToVerify: Does the server check Content-Type on POST/PUT/PATCH? Does it respect Accept headers? Does it return 415 for unsupported types?
baseContent.exampleComment: This endpoint accepts JSON but doesn't check Content-Type. A client sending XML gets a confusing parse error instead of 415.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Validate Content-Type on input. Respect Accept on output. Return 415/406 for unsupported types.

items.text: Are all non-public endpoints authenticated?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Endpoints that access user data or perform mutations must require authentication. Public endpoints should be explicitly marked.
baseContent.whyItMatters: An unauthenticated endpoint that exposes user data is a critical vulnerability. It's the most common API security issue.
baseContent.howToVerify: Is there an auth middleware? Are public endpoints explicitly opted out? Could any endpoint be accessed without a token?
baseContent.exampleComment: The `GET /users/{id}/profile` endpoint has no authentication check. Any unauthenticated request can read user profiles.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Default to authenticated. Explicitly opt out for public endpoints. Never forget auth on new routes.

items.text: Is authorization checked (not just authentication)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Authentication verifies identity. Authorization verifies the user has permission for the specific resource or action.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A user authenticated as Alice should not be able to access Bob's data. Missing authorization = data breach.
baseContent.howToVerify: After authentication, does the endpoint verify the user owns or has access to the requested resource?
baseContent.exampleComment: User A can access `GET /users/B/orders` — there's no check that the authenticated user matches the requested user ID.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always check: does THIS user have permission to access THIS resource? Authentication is not authorization.

items.text: Is CORS configured correctly (not wildcard in production)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) controls which domains can call your API from a browser. Wildcard (*) allows any origin.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A permissive CORS policy lets malicious websites make authenticated requests to your API using the user's cookies.
baseContent.howToVerify: Is CORS configured? Is the origin whitelist specific? Are credentials allowed with a wildcard origin (which browsers block)?
baseContent.exampleComment: CORS is set to `*` (all origins) with credentials enabled. This is insecure — specify the allowed origins explicitly.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Whitelist specific origins. Never use `*` with credentials. Restrict to your frontend domains.

items.text: Is rate limiting implemented?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Rate limiting prevents abuse, brute force attacks, and accidental DoS from buggy clients. Limits should vary by endpoint sensitivity.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without rate limiting, an attacker can brute-force passwords, scrape all data, or overwhelm the server with requests.
baseContent.howToVerify: Are rate limits configured? Are they per-user or per-IP? Are sensitive endpoints (login, registration) more strictly limited?
baseContent.exampleComment: The login endpoint has no rate limiting. An attacker can brute-force passwords at thousands of attempts per second.

codeExamples.label: Rate limit headers
codeExamples.code:
HTTP/1.1 429 Too Many Requests
Retry-After: 60
X-RateLimit-Limit: 100
X-RateLimit-Remaining: 0
X-RateLimit-Reset: 1704067200

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Rate limit all endpoints. Stricter limits on auth endpoints. Return Retry-After headers.

items.text: Is all input validated and sanitized?

baseContent.whatItMeans: All request input — body, query params, path params, headers — must be validated for type, format, length, and range.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unvalidated input leads to injection attacks, crashes from unexpected types, and data corruption from invalid values.
baseContent.howToVerify: Is the request body validated against a schema? Are query params type-checked? Are string lengths limited?
baseContent.exampleComment: The `name` field accepts strings of unlimited length. Add a max length of 500 to prevent storage abuse.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Validate everything at the boundary. Trust nothing from the client.

items.text: Is the error response format consistent and documented?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Error responses should follow a consistent format with a machine-readable code, human-readable message, and optional details.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Inconsistent error formats make client error handling fragile. A standard format enables reusable error handling code.
baseContent.howToVerify: Do all errors return the same structure? Is there an error code, message, and details field?
baseContent.exampleComment: Some endpoints return `{ error: 'message' }` and others return `{ message: '...', code: '...' }`. Standardize the format.

codeExamples.label: Error format

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Define one error format. Use it everywhere. Document it in the API reference.

items.text: Are stack traces and internal details excluded from error responses?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Production error responses must not include stack traces, SQL queries, file paths, or internal error messages.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Stack traces reveal framework versions, file structure, and internal logic — valuable information for attackers.
baseContent.howToVerify: Do 500 errors include stack traces? Are database error messages returned to the client? Are file paths leaked?
baseContent.exampleComment: This 500 response includes a full stack trace and the SQL query that failed. Log it server-side but return a generic error to the client.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Log details server-side. Return generic errors to clients. Never expose internals.

items.text: Do validation errors specify which fields are invalid?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Validation errors should list each invalid field with a specific error message so clients can display field-level errors.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A generic 'Invalid input' error forces users to guess what's wrong. Field-specific errors improve the user experience.
baseContent.howToVerify: Do validation errors name the field, explain the constraint, and provide actionable guidance?
baseContent.exampleComment: This returns `400 Bad Request` with no details. Return field-specific validation errors.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Validation errors should be specific: which field, what's wrong, what's expected.

items.text: Are error codes documented in the API reference?

baseContent.whatItMeans: API error codes should be documented so client developers know what errors to expect and how to handle them.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Undocumented error codes require client developers to discover them through trial and error, slowing integration.
baseContent.howToVerify: Is there a list of error codes in the API docs? Does each endpoint document its possible error responses?
baseContent.exampleComment: Document the error codes this endpoint can return: VALIDATION_ERROR, NOT_FOUND, CONFLICT, RATE_LIMITED.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Document every error code. Client developers shouldn't discover errors in production.

items.text: Is there an OpenAPI/Swagger specification?

baseContent.whatItMeans: An OpenAPI specification provides machine-readable API documentation that enables code generation, testing tools, and interactive docs.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without an OpenAPI spec, documentation is manual and quickly outdated. Tools can't auto-generate clients or validate requests.
baseContent.howToVerify: Is there an openapi.yaml or swagger.json? Is it generated from code or manually maintained? Is it up to date?
baseContent.exampleComment: Add an OpenAPI spec for this new endpoint so it appears in the API docs and can be used for client code generation.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Maintain an OpenAPI spec. Generate it from code if possible. Validate it in CI.

items.text: Are request/response examples provided for each endpoint?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Examples help developers understand the API faster than reading schemas. Real-world examples with actual data are most useful.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without examples, developers must construct requests by reading the schema, which is slower and more error-prone.
baseContent.howToVerify: Does the OpenAPI spec include example requests and responses? Are edge cases documented?
baseContent.exampleComment: Add a request/response example for this endpoint. Include both a successful response and a common error case.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Examples are worth more than schemas. Show real requests and responses for common use cases.

items.text: Are API changes documented in a changelog?

baseContent.whatItMeans: API changes should be tracked in a changelog so consumers know what changed, what's new, and what's deprecated.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without a changelog, API consumers must diff the OpenAPI spec or discover changes through broken integrations.
baseContent.howToVerify: Is there an API changelog? Does it include this change? Are breaking changes clearly marked?
baseContent.exampleComment: Add this new endpoint to the API changelog with the release version.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Keep a changelog. Note new endpoints, breaking changes, and deprecations.

items.text: Are appropriate caching headers set?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Cache-Control, ETag, and Last-Modified headers enable client-side and CDN caching, reducing server load and latency.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without caching headers, every request hits the server. Static or slowly-changing data wastes bandwidth and server resources.
baseContent.howToVerify: Are Cache-Control headers set for cacheable responses? Are ETags used for conditional requests?
baseContent.exampleComment: The product catalog endpoint is called 1000x/minute but has no caching headers. Add `Cache-Control: public, max-age=300` for 5-minute caching.

codeExamples.label: Caching headers
codeExamples.code:
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Cache-Control: public, max-age=300
ETag: "abc123"
Last-Modified: Sat, 01 Jan 2024 00:00:00 GMT

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Set Cache-Control for cacheable data. Use ETags for efficient conditional requests.

items.text: Is response compression (gzip/brotli) enabled?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Compressing JSON responses with gzip or brotli typically reduces payload size by 70-90%, improving latency and bandwidth usage.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Large JSON responses sent uncompressed waste bandwidth and increase latency, especially on mobile networks.
baseContent.howToVerify: Is compression middleware configured? Does the server respect Accept-Encoding? Are large responses being compressed?
baseContent.exampleComment: JSON responses are sent uncompressed. Enable gzip compression — it typically reduces a 100KB response to 15KB.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Enable gzip/brotli compression for all JSON responses. It's a free performance win.

items.text: Is there a batch endpoint for operations commonly done in sequence?

baseContent.whatItMeans: When clients commonly make multiple related requests, a batch endpoint reduces round trips and improves performance.
baseContent.whyItMatters: 10 sequential API calls add up to 10x the latency. A batch endpoint does the same work in one round trip.
baseContent.howToVerify: Does the client make multiple related requests that could be batched? Would a bulk create/update endpoint help?
baseContent.exampleComment: The client creates 20 line items one at a time. Add a `POST /orders/{id}/items/batch` endpoint that accepts an array.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Provide batch endpoints for operations that clients commonly do in loops.

items.text: Are responses sized appropriately (no over-fetching)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: API responses should return only the data clients need. Sparse fieldsets or different detail levels prevent over-fetching.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Returning 50 fields when the client needs 5 wastes bandwidth, especially on mobile. Large responses also slow JSON parsing.
baseContent.howToVerify: Does the response include fields the client doesn't need? Would a `?fields=id,name,email` parameter help?
baseContent.exampleComment: The list endpoint returns full objects with all nested relationships. Add a compact list representation or support sparse fieldsets.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Return appropriate detail levels. List endpoints should be lean. Detail endpoints can be full.
